structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a single hospital system spanning 5 years (60 months). Data includes all patients undergoing orthopedic surgery procedures with complete structured data (demographics, preoperative labs, medications, comorbidities), unstructured clinical notes (preoperative and intraoperative), and documented post-operative complication outcomes within 90 days of surgery.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: presence of any post-operative complication (surgical site infection, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, acute kidney injury, readmission within 30 days, or return to OR) within 90 days post-surgery, vs. no complication. Label is defined a priori using institutional complication criteria and ICD-10 codes, adjudicated by a clinician blinded to model predictions.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification. First 36 months (months 1–36) allocated to training; months 37–48 to validation; months 49–60 to test. Within each temporal window, stratify by surgeon and complication rate quartile to ensure balanced representation of surgical practices and outcome severity across splits. Temporal split is essential because orthopedic practice evolves over time, post-operative protocols change, and the model must generalize to future patients; stratification ensures confounds (surgeon variability, case complexity) are not systematically imbalanced across splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). Scaling parameters (mean, std) for structured features, imputation strategies (median for labs, mode for categorical), text vectorization vocabulary (TF-IDF or learned embeddings), and any dimensionality reduction are computed on training data only. These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. No statistics or parameters from validation or test data inform preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Structured features: age, sex, BMI, ASA score, comorbidity count, preoperative hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine, primary surgery type (one-hot encoded), surgeon identifier (as random effect or fixed effect dummy). Unstructured features: preoperative clinical notes (operative indication, comorbidities, contraindications) and intraoperative notes (operative time, blood loss, complications during procedure) are embedded using a pretrained clinical BERT model (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT) to generate fixed-length contextual representations. Derived temporal features: days from admission to surgery, duration of preoperative workup. All features represent information available before or during surgery; no post-operative temporal information is included in the feature set.
structured_choices.model: Neural network with multimodal architecture: (1) Structured data branch: 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU activation, batch norm, dropout 0.3); (2) Text embedding branch: frozen pretrained BERT embeddings → average pooling → 1–2 fully connected layers (128 units, ReLU, dropout 0.3); (3) Concatenation of both branches → 2 fully connected layers (128→64 units, ReLU, dropout 0.3) → sigmoid output. Hyperparameters (layer sizes, dropout rates, learning rate, batch size, epochs) are tuned on the validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, labs, surgery type, comorbidities, surgeon fixed effects) using identical preprocessing. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization (L1/L2) is optimized via grid search on the validation set using the same metric (see below). Critically, the baseline does NOT include text embeddings—this ensures the comparison isolates the effect of multimodal integration and neural architecture, not simply additional features. Both models use identical feature preprocessing and the same validation protocol.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Sensitivity (recall) at a fixed specificity threshold of 85%. Rationale: clinical stakeholders prioritize identifying at-risk patients to enable preventive intervention; missing a complication-prone patient (false negative) is costlier than a false alarm (false positive). A sensitivity floor ensures the model catches the majority of high-risk cases while maintaining clinician workload feasibility (specificity ≥85%). This metric is operationally aligned with the stated goal of 'reducing complication rate' by identifying candidates for enhanced monitoring. Secondary constraint: positive predictive value (PPV) must exceed 40% to ensure clinical utility.
secondary_metrics: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) — overall discrimination ability
Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) — discriminative power under class imbalance
Calibration (expected calibration error, calibration plots) — reliability of predicted probabilities for clinical risk stratification
F1 score — balance of precision and recall
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 37–48, stratified as above) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. The primary metric (sensitivity at 85% specificity) is computed on the validation set; the model checkpoint with the best validation sensitivity is selected. Early stopping (if applicable) uses validation loss or validation primary metric. No information from the test set informs model selection.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 49–60) is held completely separate and used only once, at the end of the experiment, to report final performance. Test set results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling or stratified k-fold evaluation on the test set itself). Test results are not used to adjust hyperparameters, select features, or modify the model architecture.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Surgeon identity and surgical practice variation are addressed through stratified splitting and inclusion as a fixed effect (or random intercept) in both the logistic regression baseline and neural network. Post-operative care protocols and institutional changes over the 5-year window are mitigated by the temporal split: the model is evaluated on held-out future months, simulating real deployment where practices may evolve. Subgroup analysis is performed on test data: primary metric is reported overall and stratified by surgeon quartiles, complication severity, age group (>65 vs. ≤65), and surgery type. If performance differs materially across subgroups (e.g., sensitivity >10 percentage points lower for a particular surgeon or procedure), this is documented as a limitation and generalizability concern. Missing data mechanisms are documented: if missingness is not random (e.g., certain labs omitted for sicker patients), this is noted and sensitivity analysis is performed using multiply imputed datasets.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Neural Network vs. Logistic Regression for Post-Operative Complication Prediction

### 1. Data Selection and Preparation

We would use electronic health records from a single hospital system spanning 5 years (60 months). The dataset comprises all patients undergoing orthopedic surgery with complete structured data (age, sex, BMI, ASA grade, preoperative labs including hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine), unstructured clinical text (preoperative assessment notes, operative reports, intraoperative notes), and verified post-operative outcomes. We would extract all patients with documented complications (surgical site infection, VTE, pneumonia, acute kidney injury, readmission, or return to OR) within 90 days post-surgery and balance these against uncomplicated cases.

The label is defined a priori using institutional complication criteria and validated against ICD-10 codes, with clinical adjudication by a physician blinded to model predictions to ensure consistency.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We would employ a temporal split with stratification. Months 1–36 are designated for training; months 37–48 for validation; months 49–60 for held-out test evaluation. This temporal strategy prevents information leakage and ensures the model generalizes to genuinely future patients, reflecting real-world deployment where clinical practice, protocols, and patient populations evolve.

Within each temporal window, we would stratify by surgeon (or surgical team) and complication rate quartile to ensure that surgical practice variability and case complexity are balanced across splits. This stratification mitigates confounding from individual surgeon technique, care protocols, and case selection that would otherwise correlate with outcomes.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include demographics (age, sex, BMI, ASA score), preoperative laboratory values (hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine, comorbidity burden), surgery type (one-hot encoded), and surgeon identifier (fixed effect). Unstructured clinical notes (preoperative and intraoperative) are embedded using a pretrained clinical language model (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT fine-tuned on MIMIC) to generate fixed-length contextual vector representations. Derived features include days from admission to surgery.

All preprocessing—scaling of labs, imputation of missing values, text vectorization, and dimensionality reduction—is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data. No statistics or learned parameters from validation or test sets inform preprocessing, eliminating leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The neural network employs a multimodal architecture: a structured data branch (2–3 fully connected layers with batch normalization and dropout) processes demographics and labs; a text branch (frozen pretrained embeddings → pooling → fully connected layers) processes clinical notes. Branches are concatenated and passed through additional fully connected layers, culminating in a sigmoid output.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, labs, surgery type, surgeon effects) using identical preprocessing and equivalent tuning effort. Critically, the baseline does not incorporate text embeddings. This design isolates the hypothesis: the improvement comes from multimodal integration and neural capacity, not from additional features.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both the neural network and logistic regression undergo hyperparameter optimization on the validation set (months 37–48). For the neural network, we tune layer sizes, dropout rates, learning rate, batch size, and optimization algorithm. For logistic regression, we optimize L1/L2 regularization strength. Model selection uses the primary metric (sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity) evaluated on validation data. The best model is selected and locked before test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is sensitivity (recall) at a fixed specificity of 85%. This reflects clinical priorities: identifying at-risk patients for preventive intervention is the priority. A sensitivity floor ensures broad capture of complications while maintaining operational feasibility (clinician workload). Secondary metrics include AUROC, AUPRC (appropriate under potential class imbalance), calibration curves, and F1 score.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting

The test set (months 49–60) is evaluated exactly once, at the conclusion of the experiment. Final performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap. Subgroup analysis stratifies test results by surgeon, complication severity, age, and surgery type to detect performance heterogeneity and identify generalization limitations.

### 8. Scope and Limitations

This design establishes whether the multimodal neural network achieves superior discrimination and sensitivity compared to logistic regression on structured data alone, within a single hospital system over a 5-year window. It does not establish whether the model is ready for clinical deployment (external validation on other hospitals, prospective validation, and cost-benefit analysis are required). Surgeon and practice-level confounding is mitigated through stratification and fixed effects modeling, but residual unmeasured confounding from individual surgeon skill cannot be eliminated.
